ja-gram-n3-1596-fill-0: She's asking someone to do something for her benefit — the speaker (or subject) receives the favor of being asked, so くれる fits.
ja-gram-n3-1596-fill-1: The teacher gave the book toward the speaker — the action flows from someone else down to me, which is exactly what くれる expresses.
ja-gram-n3-1596-fill-3: The friend did the buying as a favor directed at the speaker — the benefit comes inward to me, making くれる the right choice.
ja-gram-n3-1596-fill-4: Thanking someone for coming implies they did it as a favor for you — くれる frames their action as something received, adding warmth to the thanks.
ja-gram-n3-1312-fill-0: You're doing the reading for the baby's benefit, not your own — てあげる marks an action you perform as a gift or service for someone else.
ja-gram-n3-1312-fill-1: Helping neighbors is framed as something you do for their sake — てあげる signals the action is an outward gift of effort toward others.
ja-gram-n3-1312-fill-2: This is all the speaker can do for the listener — てあげる shows the action is offered as a favor directed outward to that person.
ja-gram-n3-1312-fill-3: All three tasks (shopping, cleaning, cooking) are done on your behalf — てあげる groups them together as favors the speaker is giving to you.
ja-gram-n3-1312-fill-4: Promising to take someone to the zoo is a kind act done for them — てあげる expresses that this future action is a gift to the other person.
ja-gram-n3-718-fill-0: Breaking yet another plate is an unwanted, completed mishap — the regret/completion nuance of しまう fits the speaker's frustration perfectly.
ja-gram-n3-718-fill-1: Forgetting to live is presented as a negative outcome one drifts into — しまう marks this as an undesirable completed (or recurring) state.
ja-gram-n3-718-fill-2: Being laughed at is an outcome the speaker sees as unfortunate and definite — しまう signals this undesirable result will fully come to pass.
ja-gram-n3-718-fill-3: Losing the wallet was an accidental, regrettable completed action — しまう captures both the finality and the 'oh no' feeling of the loss.
ja-gram-n3-784-fill-0: Someone intentionally left the window in an open state — てある describes an object left in a condition as a result of a purposeful action.
ja-gram-n3-784-fill-1: The window was deliberately closed and remains that way — てある shows the resulting closed state was purposely created and is still in effect.
ja-gram-n3-784-fill-2: The speaker intentionally left the window open — with を, てある focuses on the subject's deliberate action of putting something into that state.
ja-gram-n3-784-fill-3: The speaker purposely kept the window shut — てある signals the closed state was intentionally maintained, not something that just happened.
ja-gram-n3-1127-fill-0: Going to buy the newspaper implies you'll return afterward — てくる attaches to an action done away from home with the expectation of coming back.
ja-gram-n3-1127-fill-1: The speaker went to the doctor, got medicine, and came back — てきた captures both the completed away-action and the return to the speaker's base.
ja-gram-n3-1127-fill-2: Understanding grew gradually over time and has arrived at the present — てきた expresses a slow change that progressed up to now, not a sudden event.
ja-gram-n3-1127-fill-3: Prices continuing to fall describes an ongoing trend moving toward the present — てくる marks a gradual change unfolding in the current direction.
ja-gram-n3-1559-fill-0: Trying on clothes to see how they fit is a classic 'do it and see' situation — てみる expresses doing something as an experiment or trial.
ja-gram-n3-783-fill-0: Eating is happening right now at the moment of speaking — ている attaches to an action in progress to show it is currently ongoing.
ja-gram-n3-783-fill-1: Looking is happening at this moment — ている marks the action as currently in progress, the simplest ongoing-action usage of this form.
ja-gram-n3-1400-fill-0: Staying up all night to finish the book shows total completion with a sense of 'I went and did it' — てしまう marks the action as fully concluded.
ja-gram-n3-1400-fill-1: Crying at the movie was unplanned and complete — てしまった captures the 'ended up doing it' feeling of an emotional result you didn't fully intend.
ja-gram-n3-1400-fill-2: The homework is completely finished and done — てしまいました emphasizes full completion, often with a slightly proud or relieved tone.
ja-gram-n3-1400-fill-3: Falling asleep every time while reading is an unintended habitual outcome — てしまう fits this recurring, slightly regrettable result.
ja-gram-n3-1400-fill-4: He inevitably spends everything he earns on books — てしまう captures the sense that this complete, habitual spending happens as if he can't help it.
ja-gram-n3-509-fill-0: Studying Japanese happened during a general time period in Japan — 時 is the natural choice for a broad 'when' without pinpointing an exact moment.
ja-gram-n3-509-fill-1: Pay back whenever the timing works for you — 時 refers to a general, flexible time occasion rather than a specific scheduled moment.
ja-gram-n3-917-fill-0: While already visiting the hometown, the school visit was a secondary bonus trip — ついでに means you seized the opportunity while already doing something else.
ja-gram-n3-917-fill-1: Mailing the letter was a secondary task done during an existing trip to work — ついでに captures doing B efficiently while already on the way for A.
ja-gram-n3-917-fill-2: Since you're going out anyway, taking the garbage is a convenient side task — ついでに means making use of an already-planned activity to add another.
ja-gram-n3-917-fill-3: London is a side stop while already making the trip to Paris — ついでに frames dropping by as a natural bonus, not the main purpose.
ja-gram-n3-917-fill-4: The exhibition was seen opportunistically during an existing trip to the department store — ついでに shows the second activity piggybacked on the first.
ja-gram-n3-1621-fill-0: The problems stayed in their unsolved condition without changing — まま describes a state that persists unchanged from how it was.
ja-gram-n3-1621-fill-1: Keeping the house exactly as it currently is — まま expresses wanting no change to an existing state.
ja-gram-n3-1621-fill-2: The house can't sell in its current unchanged condition — まま describes the state as-is, implying it has not been improved or altered.
ja-gram-n3-1621-fill-3: The bicycle has been sitting there since early in the month, untouched — まま shows it was placed there and left in that same state ever since.
ja-gram-n3-1621-fill-4: The window has been sitting broken for a month without being fixed — まま describes the unchanged broken state continuing over that time.
ja-gram-n3-589-fill-0: Catching the son in the act of sneaking cookies happens right at that exact moment — ところ pinpoints the precise instant the action was caught in progress.
ja-gram-n3-589-fill-1: The bus was on the verge of leaving — ところだった pins the moment to just before departure, capturing the 'about to' timing.
ja-gram-n3-589-fill-3: I'm just about to begin cleaning right now — ところです marks the action as imminent, happening at precisely this moment in time.
ja-gram-n3-888-fill-0: I've just now arrived at this exact moment — ところ pinpoints the precise present instant of arrival rather than a general time.
ja-gram-n3-888-fill-2: I've only just started, right at this moment — ところ marks the exact point in time: the action just began, contrasting with 'finished.'
ja-gram-n3-888-fill-3: You found him at the specific moment he was alone with her — ところ identifies the precise scene/occasion that was discovered.
ja-gram-n3-888-fill-4: Staying 'at my place' means the speaker's physical location — ところ functions as a concrete location reference here, not a time expression.
ja-gram-n3-1737-fill-0: There's a deadline of tomorrow for finishing it — までに sets a time limit by which the action must be completed.
ja-gram-n3-1737-fill-1: Noon is the deadline for returning — までに marks a point in time that serves as the cutoff, not just a general 'when.'
ja-gram-n3-1737-fill-2: The document submission has a cutoff of evening — までに sets evening as the boundary by which the action must be done.
ja-gram-n3-1131-fill-0: Youth is a condition that won't last — うちに urges action while the current state (being young) still holds, before it changes.
ja-gram-n3-1131-fill-1: Come visit while you're still free — うちに means to act during a window of time before the condition (free time) ends.
ja-gram-n3-1131-fill-2: Accepting something 'within' the bounds of tacit understanding — うちに here expresses the implicit frame or context within which agreement happens.
ja-gram-n3-1131-fill-3: The weight gain happened so fast it was noticeable while watching — うちに captures the sense that it occurred within that brief window of observation.
ja-gram-n3-1131-fill-4: Eat it while it's still hot — うちに urges action before the current state (hotness) changes, emphasizing the limited window.
ja-gram-n3-1162-fill-0: The sister saw him at the exact moment he was sneaking a cigarette — ところを marks an action caught in progress, often with a surveillance or discovery nuance.
ja-gram-n3-1162-fill-1: The earthquake struck at the precise moment of deep sleep — ところに marks something unexpected arriving right at that specific moment.
ja-gram-n3-1162-fill-2: Just as he decided to call her, she called first — ところへ marks a new event arriving right into the middle of the existing moment.
ja-gram-n3-1162-fill-3: The officer arrested him at the exact moment of leaving the store — ところを focuses on the precise action-in-progress that was caught or intercepted.
ja-gram-n3-1162-fill-4: Thanking someone for coming despite being busy — ところを acknowledges they came at the specific moment when they were occupied, making the thanks more meaningful.
ja-gram-n3-1719-fill-0: They forgot that time was passing — 時間がたつ is the phrase for time passing, and here it's what the speaker lost track of.
ja-gram-n3-1719-fill-1: Actively waiting for time to pass — 時間がたつ is what you wait for when you're just letting time go by.
ja-gram-n3-1719-fill-2: Time passing quickly is the subject of the sentence — 時間がたつ is the expression used when commenting on how fast or slow time goes.
ja-gram-n3-1719-fill-3: Time flying when having fun is a common observation about how time passes — 時間がたつ is the phrase that captures this passage of time.
ja-gram-n3-1719-fill-4: As time passes, capacitors lose charge — 時間がたつ describes the natural process of time going by that causes the change.
ja-gram-n3-739-fill-2: The stomach growling happened throughout the meeting — ちゅう attaches to 会議 to mean 'during the entire meeting,' showing the time span.
ja-gram-n3-739-fill-4: The calls happened repeatedly during the whole overseas trip — ちゅう expresses the entire duration of travel abroad as the time frame.
ja-gram-n3-1678-fill-0: The speaker is raising my son as a (negative) topic — ときたら introduces a topic with a critical or exasperated tone, signaling a complaint is coming.
ja-gram-n3-1678-fill-1: The speaker is bringing herself up as a topic to self-criticize — ときたら singles out the subject with a negative or self-deprecating framing.
ja-gram-n3-1295-fill-0: This agenda item is placed at the center of the discussion — を中心に sets something as the focal point around which other things revolve.
ja-gram-n3-1295-fill-1: Stores have gathered around the station as their central hub — を中心に describes the station as the focal point of the surrounding commercial area.
ja-gram-n3-1295-fill-2: China is the central focus of the overseas expansion plan — を中心に places China at the core of the discussion.
ja-gram-n3-1295-fill-3: This region is the geographic center around which the mountain ranges extend — を中心 (followed by として) marks it as the axis of the spatial description.
ja-gram-n3-1295-fill-4: Aunt Mary was the central person around whom the whole household revolved — を中心に captures her role as the pivot of the family's daily life.
ja-gram-n3-585-fill-0: The room is being used in the role of a storeroom — として marks the capacity or function the room is assigned, not its inherent nature.
ja-gram-n3-585-fill-1: Mr. Lee works in the role of an engineer — として shows the professional capacity in which he is employed at the company.
ja-gram-n3-585-fill-2: He made the comment in the capacity of a joke, not seriously — として frames the statement's intended role or purpose.
ja-gram-n3-585-fill-3: Working as a teacher describes the role the speaker fills — として identifies the professional capacity, not a personal quality.
ja-gram-n3-585-fill-4: Speaking in the professional capacity of a doctor gives weight to the advice — として sets the role or standpoint from which the speaker addresses you.
ja-gram-n3-100-fill-0: A girl was born, going against what the parents had hoped for — に反して marks the outcome as directly contrary to an expectation or wish.
ja-gram-n3-100-fill-1: The government's action was the opposite of what people expected — に反して shows the action went directly against a stated expectation.
ja-gram-n3-100-fill-2: Dropping out went against the speaker's own aspiration of becoming a scientist — に反して shows the outcome opposed a personal goal.
ja-gram-n3-100-fill-3: Destroying nature goes against God's will — に反して frames humans' behavior as directly in opposition to a stated will or intention.
ja-gram-n3-100-fill-4: Prices rising weekly is the opposite of what the speaker was hoping for — に反して marks the outcome as going contrary to a personal expectation.
ja-gram-n3-1249-fill-0: Columbus is the agent who performed the discovery — によって in passive sentences identifies who carried out the action.
ja-gram-n3-1249-fill-1: Mr. Dan Sman is the author who wrote the book — によって marks the agent responsible for creating or producing something.
ja-gram-n3-1249-fill-2: Taking a plane is the method used to make up for lost time — によって marks the means or method by which the result is achieved.
ja-gram-n3-1249-fill-3: Words are the means by which thoughts get expressed — によって marks the medium or tool through which something is communicated.
ja-gram-n3-1249-fill-4: She is the agent who performed the cutting — によって identifies the person who carried out the passive action.
ja-gram-n3-972-fill-0: The employment term is set in formal compliance with the contract — に従い is the formal written variant of に従って, fitting a legal/official context.
ja-gram-n3-972-fill-1: Spending rises in step with income — に従って describes a parallel change where one thing increases in accordance with another.
ja-gram-n3-522-fill-0: Japan's foreign policy is directed toward foreign affairs — に対して marks the target or addressee of a policy or stance.
ja-gram-n3-522-fill-1: Entering university in Japan is contrasted with graduating in America — に対して sets up a direct comparison between two opposing situations.
ja-gram-n3-522-fill-2: Citizens direct their opposition toward the tax hike — に対して marks the target of their reaction or attitude.
ja-gram-n3-522-fill-3: The speaker's interest is directed toward politics as its target — に対して identifies what the strong interest is aimed at.
ja-gram-n3-522-fill-4: The 500-yen fee is calculated in proportion to each 10,000 yen — に対して expresses a rate or ratio relative to a given quantity.
ja-gram-n3-117-fill-0: Teaching at this university is an honor from the speaker's personal perspective — にとって marks the viewpoint from which something is evaluated.
ja-gram-n3-117-fill-1: The factory closure is a life-or-death matter from the townspeople's perspective — にとって marks whose point of view makes this a critical issue.
ja-gram-n3-117-fill-2: This is the safest place judged from your particular situation — にとって marks you as the reference point for what counts as safe.
ja-gram-n3-117-fill-3: Dairy being hard to eat is a judgment from the perspective of Asians — にとって marks the group whose experience makes this true.
ja-gram-n3-117-fill-4: The photo being most important is an assessment from the speaker's personal standpoint — にとって marks whose values and feelings define the importance.
ja-gram-n3-308-fill-0: The story is being adapted to suit children as its target audience — 向き means aimed at or appropriate for a specific group.
ja-gram-n3-308-fill-1: These books are not aimed at or suitable for young readers — 向き judges fit or appropriateness for a target audience.
ja-gram-n3-308-fill-2: The plain English makes the book suitable and aimed toward beginners — 向き signals the intended or appropriate audience for something.
ja-gram-n3-308-fill-3: The text is designed with beginners as the intended audience — 向き describes who the material is built for or aimed at.
ja-gram-n3-308-fill-4: The flashy dress doesn't suit or fit the speaker's style — 向き here means appropriate for or fitting for a particular person.
ja-gram-n3-976-fill-0: The hiring decision hinges on the outcome — によって (depending on) shows the result varies conditional on that factor.
ja-gram-n3-976-fill-1: Whether the event is cancelled depends on the weather — によって marks weather as the variable factor the decision hinges on.
ja-gram-n3-976-fill-2: The question already has によう in it; the blank completes って to finish によって — it marks the financial situation as the deciding variable.
ja-gram-n3-976-fill-3: Kanji meaning varies depending on which character is used — によって marks the kanji as the varying factor that changes the meaning.
ja-gram-n3-976-fill-4: Coming or not depends entirely on the weather — によって marks the variable condition that determines the outcome.
ja-gram-n3-1305-fill-0: Knowing facts about Japan is the topic being reflected on — について introduces the subject matter under discussion or consideration.
ja-gram-n3-1305-fill-1: The new software release is the topic to be touched on — について marks what the speaker intends to address at the banquet.
ja-gram-n3-1305-fill-2: The plan is the subject being referred to — について attaches to a noun to signal it as the topic of discussion or inquiry.
ja-gram-n3-1305-fill-3: Going there is the topic under consideration — について marks the act of going as the subject matter being discussed.
ja-gram-n3-1305-fill-4: Computer usage is the topic the desired book would cover — について marks the subject matter the book should be about.
ja-gram-n3-1679-fill-0: Viewing the town happens via the lens as an intermediary medium — を通して marks the lens as the channel or instrument of perception.
ja-gram-n3-1679-fill-1: Reality is perceived by passing through one's own eyes — を通して marks the eyes as the medium of experience.
ja-gram-n3-1679-fill-2: The order is placed by routing it through an intermediary — を通して marks the go-between as the channel the transaction passes through.
ja-gram-n3-1679-fill-3: The discussions are conducted using the interpreter as the communication channel — を通して marks the interpreter as the medium connecting both sides.
ja-gram-n3-1679-fill-4: The information about her reputation came via Mrs. Tanaka as a source — を通して marks her as the intermediary channel through which it was learned.
ja-gram-n3-882-fill-2: Keeping in touch with Mr. Smith is a mutual, back-and-forth action — 合う adds the 'with each other' dimension to the verb 取り合う.
ja-gram-n3-882-fill-3: Understanding one another requires both sides to act mutually — 合う turns 理解する into a reciprocal action between people.
ja-gram-n3-882-fill-4: 付き合う means to keep company or associate with someone — 合う is the core component that makes this a mutual relationship verb.
ja-gram-n3-1626-fill-1: The company published a book in the past — 出した is the past tense form used here for the act of issuing or putting out something.
ja-gram-n3-1626-fill-2: Taking out the garbage is the act of removing it from a space — 出す here means to put out or remove, not a verb-stem compound.
ja-gram-n3-1626-fill-3: Paying the money first is the condition before buying — 出す means to put out or produce money, used for paying or providing funds.
ja-gram-n3-800-fill-1: She keeps living that unfulfilling life without stopping — 続ける attaches to a verb stem to show the action continues without ceasing.
ja-gram-n3-800-fill-2: People will go on talking about this mistake indefinitely — 続ける marks the talking as an action that keeps on going into the future.
ja-gram-n3-800-fill-3: I have always loved and will continue to love my country — 続ける shows the action (loving) persists without interruption over time.
ja-gram-n3-800-fill-4: The command is to keep writing without stopping — 続け is the て-form stem here, ordering someone to carry on with the action.
ja-gram-n3-1685-fill-0: Failing the first time means retaking the exam is doing the same action over again — 直す means to redo or repeat an action, often to correct a previous result.
ja-gram-n3-1203-fill-0: She went out in a coatless state — なし attaches to a noun to mean 'without that thing,' describing the absence of the coat.
ja-gram-n3-1203-fill-1: She left wearing no coat — なし marks the coat as something absent, characterizing the state she was in when she went out.
ja-gram-n3-1561-fill-0: Coming to school without eating breakfast means the eating was skipped — ずに attaches to a verb in negative form to mean 'without doing' that action.
ja-gram-n3-1561-fill-1: Taking the test without having studied means studying was skipped — ずに marks studying as the action that was bypassed before the test.
ja-gram-n3-1673-fill-0: Employees are happy because they don't have to ride crowded trains — なくてもすむ means they can get by, or manage fine, without having to do it.
ja-gram-n3-1673-fill-1: The easy test means you can manage without studying much — なくてもすむ shows you can get through the situation without needing to do that action.
ja-gram-n3-1785-fill-0: Lack of ability is expressed by nominalizing 'no ability' into a noun phrase — のなさ turns an adjective or verb into a noun meaning 'the absence of X.'
ja-gram-n3-1493-fill-0: Bought only a year ago, yet already outdated — なのに expresses frustration at the gap between the expected and actual situation.
ja-gram-n3-1493-fill-1: The information should be current but isn't — なのに highlights the surprising or unsatisfying contrast between what should be and what is.
ja-gram-n3-1493-fill-2: New shoes needing repair is surprising and annoying — なのに conveys the speaker's frustration that reality doesn't match expectations.
ja-gram-n3-1493-fill-3: Having walked all the way implies strong legs — なのに connects the evidence to the surprised conclusion in a logical-contrast chain.
ja-gram-n3-1493-fill-4: Jim realized he was alone — なのに here introduces the surprising condition he found himself in upon waking.
ja-gram-n3-1368-fill-1: As soon as leaving the house, rain started — と marks an immediate, automatic sequence where one event naturally triggers the next.
ja-gram-n3-1368-fill-2: Buying here automatically gives you the price range — と shows the natural consequence that follows from being at this location.
ja-gram-n3-1368-fill-3: Winter coming reliably brings rain — と expresses a natural, recurring conditional where the first event predictably leads to the second.
ja-gram-n3-1368-fill-4: Arriving at the station automatically triggers the need to call — と shows the natural next step that follows as a direct consequence.
ja-gram-n3-551-fill-0: Learning Japanese requires time — のに marks learning Japanese as the purpose or goal for which time is needed.
ja-gram-n3-551-fill-1: A good time and place are needed in order to meet you — のに marks meeting as the goal the time and place are required for.
ja-gram-n3-551-fill-2: A passport is what you need in order to travel overseas — のに marks traveling as the purpose the passport is necessary for.
ja-gram-n3-551-fill-3: Swimwear is used for the purpose of swimming — のに marks swimming as the function the item serves.
ja-gram-n3-551-fill-4: Five months were needed in order to complete the job — のに marks completing the job as the purpose that required all that time.
ja-gram-n3-660-fill-0: I don't eat meat (but I eat fish) — は creates a contrast, singling out meat as the item being negated while implying other foods are fine.
ja-gram-n3-660-fill-1: She's alive (but not well) — は creates the contrast between being alive and the unstated implication that all is not okay.
ja-gram-n3-660-fill-2: Liking to go to baseball is set in contrast to what was just said — は marks it as the contrasting element being introduced.
ja-gram-n3-660-fill-3: I like addition but dislike subtraction — は marks the speaker as the topic, and the two preferences are set in contrast.
ja-gram-n3-660-fill-4: Living in Shanghai placed him apart from the war — は marks him as the topic, contrasting his location with where the war was happening.
ja-gram-n3-1481-fill-0: その反面 is a fixed phrase meaning 'on the other hand' — 反面 introduces a contrasting side to what was just said.
ja-gram-n3-1481-fill-1: The brother is kind, but on the other hand has a mean side — 反面 presents the second trait as the flip side of the first.
ja-gram-n3-1481-fill-2: The job pays well, but on the other hand demands 12 hours — 反面 introduces the negative trade-off that comes with the positive.
ja-gram-n3-1481-fill-3: Strong against water but weak to heat — 反面 sets up the contrasting weakness on the other side of the strength.
ja-gram-n3-1481-fill-4: The internet is useful for learning, but on the other hand poses risks — 反面 introduces the contrasting danger that accompanies the benefit.
ja-gram-n3-1289-fill-0: He looks young considering his age as an old person — にしては sets up an expectation based on the category (being old) that the observation defies.
ja-gram-n3-1289-fill-1: Driving an old car is unexpected for someone rich — にしては judges the behavior against what you'd expect given the person's status.
ja-gram-n3-1289-fill-2: Cold mornings are unexpected for June — にしては frames June as the standard we measure against and calls the coldness surprising.
ja-gram-n3-1289-fill-3: Being vigorous at 70 defies the expected limitations of that age — にしては uses the age as the benchmark against which the vigor is surprising.
ja-gram-n3-1289-fill-4: The hotel is pricey relative to what you'd expect for this town — にしては measures the price against the expected norm for the town.
ja-gram-n3-410-fill-0: Even if marrying her were possible, it still wouldn't happen soon — としても grants the hypothesis and shows the conclusion still holds.
ja-gram-n3-410-fill-1: Even if parents are famous, personal ability is what matters — としても concedes the premise to argue that the outcome would be the same anyway.
ja-gram-n3-410-fill-2: Even if the person helped you is a subordinate, you should thank them — としても concedes the condition to reinforce that the conclusion stands regardless.
ja-gram-n3-410-fill-3: Even in the role of a gaming OS, Linux continues to evolve — としても concedes the framing to show the statement holds true even in that context.
ja-gram-n3-410-fill-4: Even if he's a nice person, the speaker still doesn't trust him — としても concedes the good quality while the contrasting conclusion stands firm.
ja-gram-n3-1593-fill-0: If only today's weather had been as nice — のに at the end of a sentence expresses a wish or longing for an unrealized situation.
ja-gram-n3-1593-fill-1: If only the rain would stop — のに expresses a heartfelt wish for something to be different from the current situation.
